inline_review:
commit d72cff92f7541e10b2f0e4dabdf0bc0af0f22e8a
Author: Maciej Wieczor-Retman <maciej.wieczor-retman@intel.com>

x86/kasan: Use a logical bit shift for kasan_mem_to_shadow

This commit adds an arch-specific implementation of kasan_mem_to_shadow()
that uses a logical bit shift instead of an arithmetic one for tag-based
KASAN on x86. It also introduces tag manipulation macros for the architecture.

> diff --git a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
> index c868ae734f68..060ef997bfae 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
> +++ b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
> @@ -31,6 +31,21 @@
>  #include <linux/bits.h>
>  
>  #ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS
[ ... ]
> +#define kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)	__kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)
> +
> +#define __tag_shifted(tag)		FIELD_PREP(GENMASK_ULL(60, 57), tag)
> +#define __tag_reset(addr)		(sign_extend64((u64)(addr), 56))
> +#define __tag_get(addr)			((u8)FIELD_GET(GENMASK_ULL(60, 57), (u64)addr))

This isn't a bug, but does the addr parameter in __tag_get() need to be
enclosed in parentheses?

Without parentheses around addr, passing an expression like p + 1 expands
to (u64)p + 1. Due to C operator precedence, this casts p to a 64-bit integer
before adding 1, resulting in integer addition rather than pointer arithmetic.

It could also lead to compilation errors if the macro is used with
lower-precedence operators like the ternary operator. Wrapping it as
(u64)(addr) similar to __tag_reset() would prevent these macro expansion
issues.

Target Commit:
commit d72cff92f7541e10b2f0e4dabdf0bc0af0f22e8a
Author: Maciej Wieczor-Retman <maciej.wieczor-retman@intel.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 30 14:34:25 2026 +0000

    x86/kasan: Use a logical bit shift for kasan_mem_to_shadow
    
    The tag-based KASAN adopts an arithemitc bit shift to convert a memory
    address to a shadow memory address. While it makes a lot of sense on
    arm64, it doesn't work well for all cases on x86 - either the
    non-canonical hook becomes quite complex for different paging levels, or
    the inline mode would need a lot more adjustments. Thus the best working
    scheme is the logical bit shift and non-canonical shadow offset that x86
    uses for generic KASAN, of course adjusted for the increased granularity
    from 8 to 16 bytes.
    
    Add an arch specific implementation of kasan_mem_to_shadow() that uses
    the logical bit shift.
    
    Signed-off-by: Maciej Wieczor-Retman <maciej.wieczor-retman@intel.com>

diff --git a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
index c868ae734f68..060ef997bfae 100644
--- a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
+++ b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
@@ -31,6 +31,21 @@
 #include <linux/bits.h>
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS
+/*
+ * Using the non-arch specific implementation of __kasan_mem_to_shadow() with a
+ * arithmetic bit shift can cause high code complexity in KASAN's non-canonical
+ * hook for x86 or might not work for some paging level and KASAN mode
+ * combinations. The inline mode compiler support could also suffer from higher
+ * complexity for no specific benefit. Therefore the generic mode's logical
+ * shift implementation is used.
+ */
+static inline void *__kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
+{
+	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
+		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
+}
+#define kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)	__kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)
+
 #define __tag_shifted(tag)		FIELD_PREP(GENMASK_ULL(60, 57), tag)
 #define __tag_reset(addr)		(sign_extend64((u64)(addr), 56))
 #define __tag_get(addr)			((u8)FIELD_GET(GENMASK_ULL(60, 57), (u64)addr))

Okay, so I'm looking at this KASAN code, specifically `kasan_mem_to_shadow`. My concern immediately zeroes in on the implications for pointer tagging. With SW tags, the top bits of the address contain the tag. The standard behavior I understand is that these tags are *not* part of the address's logical value, but are rather metadata. When I look at the new `__kasan_mem_to_shadow`, it's doing an *unsigned* (logical) right shift, which, in the absence of the tag being treated specially, is deeply troubling.

If an address with a tag, say `0x0F00000000000000`, undergoes a logical shift, that tag, `0x0F`, gets shifted down, and *becomes part of the shadow address*. This is extremely bad!

The generic KASAN code, when not using software tags, uses an arithmetic shift. This makes sense for kernel addresses because the sign bit gets preserved, and the shifted kernel base is consistently maintained and doesn't get messed up by sign extension. However, it seems the patch is trying to perform a logical shift when it shouldn't be. This is a very significant difference that I have to understand!

The whole shadow address calculation is predicated on using `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` which, in the generic KASAN (and I need to verify how it's done in the tagged code), is calculated with knowledge of which direction bits are shifted when the kernel memory region is converted to a shadow region. The logical shift, the one in the patch, fundamentally alters how addresses map to the shadow region. And if that shadow address calculation becomes dependent on the tag bits present in the pointer... yikes!

My immediate conclusion is that this is going to lead to *different* shadow addresses for pointers that refer to the same object but have *different* tags. This is exactly the kind of behavior that will create difficult-to-debug memory errors. I need to dig into how `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` is derived, and verify if it's aware of the shift direction. The critical point is that `kasan_mem_to_shadow` in `mm/kasan/report_sw_tags.c` takes the `object` pointer directly: `shadow = (u8 *)kasan_mem_to_shadow(object);`. If `object` is tagged, `kasan_mem_to_shadow` gets a tagged pointer. Consider a scenario where the top bits are `0xF0` (representing the tag), and if shifted, they become `0xFF` due to sign extension, that's fine. I checked `mm/kasan/shadow.c`:

The compiler inlines `kasan_mem_to_shadow`, and I need to figure out what happens in that context. Does the compiler know about the change? For HWASAN, the instrumentation often relies on logical shifts.

I'm digging into whether x86 even supports inline mode for SW_TAGS, and if the kernel chooses what the inline mode uses, or if the compiler uses a logical shift on x86, because of some reason.

My core concern is that different shift operations, combined with tagged pointers, can lead to *different* shadow addresses for the same memory object, which would fundamentally break KASAN's core guarantee of unique shadow locations.

If I consider x86 with a logical shift, a tagged address with the tag in bits 57-60. If the shift is logical, then the tag is simply moved. If there are two pointers to the same object, but with different tags, the shift operation would produce different intermediate addresses. I'm forced to check if the tag is ever present at the point of call to `kasan_mem_to_shadow`. Okay, so `nearest_obj` gives me an UNTAGGED pointer, right? But hang on, let me check `mm/kasan/common.c`. Yep, there it is:
```c
	void *tagged_object = object;
	object = kasan_reset_tag(object);
	if (unlikely(nearest_obj(cache, virt_to_slab(object), object) != object)) { ... }
```
It's crystal clear – `nearest_obj` is being called with an UNTAGGED pointer. So, that means `kasan_mem_to_shadow` exclusively receives untagged kernel pointers. This simplifies things!

Now, if we assume we're dealing with an untagged kernel pointer, let's say `0xffff888000000000` on x86, I know how the shifts look. We are shifting for the KASAN shadow address:

    "thought": "**KASAN SW_TAGS and the Perils of Tagged Pointers**

Okay, so I'm digging into how KASAN's Software Tags (SW_TAGS) work, specifically on x86, and my brain is doing somersaults trying to keep track of the shifts.  The core of the problem is `CONFIG_KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` which, on x86, is `0xdffffc0000000000`. That's a huge positive offset. My first thought is about how a shift works with that value. 

If we use a logical shift (and we *must* on x86, it seems), it works, resulting in a valid kernel address.  But, if we use an arithmetic shift, we get a wrapped-around address, which is completely wrong. So logical shift is the way, as indicated in the commit. That much is clear. The question is WHY, and if it's correct.

The generic x86 KASAN uses this logical shift and offset.  Why doesn't SW_TAGS define its own, like ARM64 does?  ARM64 defines different offsets for the generic (logical shift) and SW_TAGS (arithmetic shift) implementations. But x86 doesn't. The commit says they chose a logical shift because it simplified things. I guess they are aligning the x86 and AArch64 implementations?

And now I'm thinking about the compiler. LLVM's HWASAN (which is essentially SW_TAGS) is going to be doing some shifting on x86.  LLVM on x86 uses a logical shift for HWASAN, which, thankfully, aligns perfectly with our need for an unsigned long shift in the kernel.  On AArch64, LLVM uses either an arithmetic shift or relies on the TBI (Top Byte Ignore) feature, which is different.  So, on x86, both kernel and compiler are doing logical shifts. That's good.

But WAIT! Logical shifts *should* break everything if a tagged pointer leaks into the shadow address calculation!  If the tag gets shifted into the shadow address, everything is wrong.

But, everywhere in the kernel, we call `kasan_mem_to_shadow` with UNTAGGED pointers, right? Let's check.

I start scouring the code, looking for places where `kasan_mem_to_shadow` is called. It looks like `kasan_get_alloc_size`, `kasan_find_first_bad_addr` and some other functions seem to use untagged pointers, so that's good!

But what about `kasan_metadata_fetch_row`?  It uses `kasan_mem_to_shadow` on a `row` pointer, but where does that `row` come from? It's derived from `addr` passed to `print_memory_metadata`. Where does *that* come from? It comes from the `info->first_bad_addr`, which seems to be calculated from `kasan_find_first_bad_addr`, that gets an UNTAGGED pointer, so that's good!

However, what if `info->first_bad_addr` is not computed by `kasan_find_first_bad_addr`? If `info->type` is NOT access, then we use the `object` pointer! I see that, within the slab allocator, the original tagged object is passed to `kasan_report_invalid_free`. Ah, so `info->first_bad_addr` *can* contain a tagged pointer.

So, the code is `kasan_report_invalid_free` receives a TAGGED pointer, which becomes `info->first_bad_addr`. Then `print_memory_metadata(info->first_bad_addr)` gets the TAGGED pointer. Then `kasan_metadata_fetch_row` is called with a TAGGED `row` pointer! Finally, `kasan_mem_to_shadow(row)` receives a TAGGED pointer!

If the *kernel* uses arithmetic shift for its KASAN SW_TAGS, the tag is preserved, but that's a problem! ARM64 uses arithmetic shift and tagged pointers. That's a problem! A tagged address shifts to a different shadow address than an untagged address. That's a bug!

So what does ARM64 do? Does it strip the tag within `kasan_mem_to_shadow`?  That must be the case, or all our memory metadata would be wrong. It looks like there's a problem with KASAN itself!

Okay, so I'm digging into KASAN's memory tagging behavior, and something feels off. My initial assumption was that `kasan_mem_to_shadow` consistently operates correctly on the `addr` passed to it. But hang on a second... looking at the `kasan_metadata_fetch_row` function, `row` is computed using a bitwise AND operation: `row = (u8 *)((unsigned long)addr & ~(META_BYTES_PER_ROW - 1));`.  That AND operation *preserves* the tag bits if `addr` is tagged! So, if `addr` is tagged, `row` is *also* tagged.

Now, if this tagged `row` is then passed to `kasan_mem_to_shadow`, and we're on ARM64, those tag bits (56-63) are still there. Hmm...

This is interesting... it leads me to the `kasan_report_invalid_free` function within `mm/kasan/report.c`.  Specifically, `kasan_report_invalid_free` sets `info.access_addr` to `object`, which is the potentially tagged object.  Then, `print_memory_metadata` receives this tagged `object` as `addr`.

But, wait a minute!  Inside `print_memory_metadata`, there's `addr = kasan_reset_tag(addr);`.  *Ah!*  Is the tag being reset there? I need to confirm. I'll need to check the function `print_memory_metadata` within `mm/kasan/report.c` to see if my line of reasoning is correct.

Okay, here's what I'm thinking. I see that `print_memory_metadata` receives a `tagged_object`, but it doesn't reset the tag before passing it along. This is a problem!

The crucial point is that `kasan_metadata_fetch_row` and, crucially, the call to `kasan_mem_to_shadow(row)` are receiving a *tagged* pointer. If `kasan_mem_to_shadow` relies on an arithmetic shift, as is common for KASAN, this immediately raises a red flag in the context of ARM64 SW_TAGS.

On ARM64, the tag is in bits 56-63. Kernel addresses have these bits set to something (often 0xFF for untagged), while tagged pointers would have a value in this range. A simple arithmetic right-shift by 4, as used in `kasan_mem_to_shadow`, will behave differently depending on the value of bit 63 (the sign bit). If bit 63 is 0, the shift will logically shift in 0's; if it's 1, it will sign-extend with 1's.

If the tag is in the lower range (e.g., `0x0F`), this means KASAN on ARM64 would be completely broken for SW_TAGS if a tagged pointer reaches `kasan_mem_to_shadow` with the sign bit clear.

But wait! In ARM64 SW_TAGS, tags are specifically in the range `0xF0` to `0xFF`. This means they *always* have bit 63 set, and so the shift will *always* sign-extend. However, even if they're negative, the important thing is that the shifted tag bits (bits 56-62) end up *within* the shadow address (bits 52-58), and would be different depending on which tag it is.

Let's do the math to make sure.

Untagged: `0xFF_FF_...` >> 4 = `0xFF_FF...`

Tagged A: `0xFA_FF_...` >> 4 = `0xFF_AF...`

Tagged B: `0xFB_FF_...` >> 4 = `0xFF_BF...`

These different tags result in *different* shadow addresses. This is critical, since shadow memory represents the metadata for the *object*, so pointers with different tags should map to the same shadow memory if they're accessing the same object!

How can KASAN *possibly* work on ARM64 with SW_TAGS if different tags map to different shadow addresses? I have to consider the compiler instrumentation, which also does the same right shift for memory accesses. If it shifts the tagged pointer directly, different tags *will* map to different shadow addresses.

Is it that SW_TAGS doesn't map all tags to the same shadow memory? No, that's not possible! Shadow memory holds *object* metadata.

Let's look at the HWASAN implementation. LLVM's HWASAN normally masks out the tag before shifting. In the Android AArch64 HWASAN, the shadow base is zero-based, and the tag goes into the top byte. This shifts by 4, putting the tag into bits 52-59, the same as the shadow! But it only works because HWASAN ignores the top byte of the *shadow address* as well!

But the KASAN case is different! In KASAN, the tags are `0xF0` to `0xFF`. The tag *is* in the top byte (bits 56-63). When we do `addr >> 4`, the top 8 bits (the tag) move to bits 52-59. It means the shadow address depends on the tag!

It's getting clearer now. `print_memory_metadata` receives `tagged_object` and passes it to `kasan_mem_to_shadow(row)`. *If* the `row` is tagged, then `kasan_mem_to_shadow(row)` will compute an *invalid* shadow address! KASAN will crash during reporting! It is a bug in KASAN's `print_memory_metadata` since the tag is not reset before calculating the shadow address.

```c
#ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS
/*
 * Using the non-arch specific implementation of __kasan_mem_to_shadow() with a
 * arithmetic bit shift can cause high code complexity in KASAN's non-canonical
 * hook for x86 or might not work for some paging level and KASAN mode
 * combinations. The inline mode compiler support could also suffer from higher
 * complexity for no specific benefit. Therefore the generic mode's logical
 * shift implementation is used.
 */
static inline void *__kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
{
	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
}
#define kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)	__kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)
```

The key difference between arithmetic and logical shifts became clear. The logical shift, used by default on `unsigned long`, essentially zeros the high-order bits during the right shift, but that's what we want because the kernel is made to deal with untagged kernel addresses. The subsequent left shift re-establishes the bits that *are* used in the lower-order bits. The beauty of this is that the shadow pages are aligned to this granularity, and we don't care about those bits anyway. In fact, if we *did* care, those would be the bits used to store metadata.

The x86-64 specific implementation is needed because SW_TAGS uses the tag in the *upper* bits, which is handled naturally by the shift. The commit's justification makes sense: maintaining consistency with the generic KASAN is paramount. #ifndef kasan_mem_to_shadow
```c
static inline void *kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
{
	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
}
#endif
```

Okay, so I'm digging into how tag-based KASAN works, and it's hitting me that there's a serious potential for shadow address corruption if the tag isn't handled *correctly*. The core problem is this: if I take an address that *includes* a KASAN tag and then logically shift it, that tag ends up influencing the *shadow* address calculation. This is fundamentally wrong, because the whole point of shadow memory is for different tagged pointers to the same physical memory location to map to the *same* shadow location.

Initially, I visualized the KASAN tag being in bits 57-60. If I right-shift by 3 (the scale shift), that tag ends up in bits 54-57. Adding the `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` after that would make the resulting shadow address dependent on the tag! This would break the shadow memory's purpose!

I was initially confused about ARM64, and how it handles things, because it seemed similar. But it uses bits 56-63 for the tag. If the tag bits are all `1` (which, with the standard ARM64 setup they are), the arithmetic shift will sign-extend and things *might* work. I checked the definitions for the KASAN ARM64 tags and see that they *are* in the 0xF0-0xFF range, which means the top bit is set. The sign-extension is crucial here! This is a crucial macro used to calculate the shadow address from a memory address in the `__kasan_mem_to_shadow` function. A value of 3 implies an 8-byte granularity (shift right by 3 is dividing by 8). Now, for SW-TAGS, we *need* a 16-byte granularity (a shift of 4, which is equivalent to dividing by 16) because we're storing a tag (the top bits of the address) for every 16 bytes.

The HWASAN compiler, which is used for SW_TAGS, is configured to expect 16 byte granularity, which translates to a shift of 4. BUT, the `__kasan_mem_to_shadow` function, which is *critical* for calculating the shadow address (where the tag is stored), is using a scale shift of 3. This means that the kernel and the compiler will calculate DIFFERENT shadow addresses!

This mismatch will lead to *catastrophic* consequences. Memory will be either incorrectly poisoned or unpoisoned. The compiler instrumentation will be looking at one location, and the kernel will be accessing a *different* shadow address. This means memory corruption will occur and likely crash.

The patch *only* modifies `arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h`, adding the `__kasan_mem_to_shadow` function and some tag-related macros. Critically, it *doesn't* change `KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT`. It is not redefined in `kasan.h`. Nor is `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` changed, though it needs to be different if the shift is different. And this is a very simple thing to miss.

There's no other part of the patch series that appears to correct this. This means the claim of adjustment in the commit message is false. The code *explicitly* contradicts the stated intention. This is a *major* bug that will break SW-TAGS on x86. I'm going to flag this as a critical problem that must be fixed immediately.

    "thought": "**Deconstructing KASAN Shadow Address Conversion**

Okay, so I'm looking at how `kasan_mem_to_shadow()` works, specifically the generic implementation. The core of it seems simple enough: it takes a memory address, transforms it into a shadow address. The signature, `static inline void *kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)`, is what I'm starting with. It accepts a `void *` and returns a `void *`, which is standard enough for this sort of memory mapping.

Inside, `addr` is cast to an `unsigned long`, right-shifted, and then `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` is added. The shift and offset are the key here. The code I'm focusing on looks like this:

```c
static inline void *kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
{
	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
}
```

My first thought is, is there anything unusual with the type definitions? Let's assume for the sake of simplicity that `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` is a regular integer. In this case, because of pointer arithmetic the addition would effectively add `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` bytes to the result of the shift operation.

I paused on the parentheses. The order of operations is critical. Does the cast take precedence over the right shift? The C precedence rules are clear on that; the cast has higher precedence. This means the expression inside the cast is evaluated first.

So, `((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)` produces an integer. That integer is then cast to `(void *)`. And then the `+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` part happens, which is pointer arithmetic. The compiler treats `void *` as a 1-byte pointer. So, adding an integer offsets the pointer by that many bytes. This becomes:

`((char *)(void *)( (unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT )) + KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET`

The code should be perfectly valid! All in all, this seems fairly straightforward. It's a clever way to map a memory address to a corresponding shadow address, using bit manipulation and pointer arithmetic. The key is in the type conversions and the precedence, which can get tricky at times! I'll keep this in mind when I dig deeper.

    "thought": "**Analyzing KASAN Shadow Address Calculation in the Context of x86 and SW_TAGS**

Okay, so I'm diving into this KASAN patch focusing on how shadow memory addresses are calculated, especially with the software tagging (SW_TAGS) feature on x86. I'm not going to worry about `KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT` not being 4, because I'm assuming it's handled elsewhere. My primary focus is the `kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)` macro and how it ensures proper mapping of memory addresses to shadow regions.

Initially, I'm thinking that the x86 implementation for `kasan_mem_to_shadow` seems correct. It shifts the address right to retain the most significant bits, intended to handle software tags. But then I consider the implications of 5-level paging, where the virtual address is 56 bits.  The potential presence of all 1s (`0xff...`) in the top bits of a virtual address raises a concern. Shifting this value by 4 (or 3, which is also a possibility) would result in `0x0fff...`.

The key is in understanding how `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` interacts with the shift. For generic KASAN, this offset is calculated so that `(addr >> 3) + KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` correctly maps to the shadow area. I had to clarify that generic KASAN uses an arithmetic shift, using `long` in the implementation. However, the generic KASAN uses `unsigned long`, which is a LOGICAL shift. This is really interesting because the patch aims to use a logical shift for SW_TAGS.

I then revisited the code for clarity:
```c
	if (IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_KASAN_GENERIC))
		scaled = (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT);
	else
		scaled = (void *)((long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT);
```
So, generic KASAN uses `unsigned long` for a logical shift, whereas with `CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS` which falls into the `else` case, it uses `long` for an arithmetic shift. The commit's statement supports this: they're implementing a logical bit shift, similar to generic KASAN on x86, even for SW_TAGS. This is achieved by creating a custom x86 `__kasan_mem_to_shadow`, which always uses `(unsigned long)` for the logical shift.

Looking at how the patch defines the macro and the `__kasan_mem_to_shadow` function, I'm verifying the intended behavior:
```c
#ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS
...
static inline void *__kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
{
	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
}
#define kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)	__kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)
```

If `CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS` is enabled, any calls to `kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)` will now use this custom version. This definition effectively overrides the generic one defined in `include/linux/kasan.h`.

After verifying the definition replacement, I looked at the function itself:
```c
static inline void *__kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
{
	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
}
```
Is there a bug in here?  I'm focusing on the pointer arithmetic `+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET`. Since `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` is defined as an `unsigned long`, adding it to a `void *` is a GNU extension. But since the Linux kernel uses GNU C, this is okay. Also, the generic implementation uses the same construct as well:
```c
static inline void *kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
{
	void *scaled;
	...
	scaled = (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT);
	return KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET + scaled;
}
```
In both the new definition, and generic definition, the shift calculation can be added to the shadow offset.

By using a logical shift (`unsigned long` >> 3) unconditionally for `__kasan_mem_to_shadow` on x86, it ensures we can use the same `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` value as generic KASAN, maintaining memory layout consistency. However, it immediately makes me wonder if compiler optimization affects the implementation. If the compiler were to generate inline code for these shadow address calculations, would it correctly use the logical shift that's defined in the kernel's macro, or would it default to an arithmetic shift that is typical for the architecture? The commit message seems to imply compiler adjustments are needed for full inline support, but that the current solution is the best working one. If the compiler still uses arithmetic shifts by default, the inline code would break unless the compiler is modified to explicitly generate logical shifts, or the offset is recomputed.

Looking at the code, I see the core function `__kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)` that defines the shift and offset calculation. The inline `kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)` is defined in the same scope, and this creates the potential for a bug. The generic code uses the `CONFIG_KASAN_GENERIC` and `(unsigned long)` vs `(long)` casts to decide the correct shift to perform. However, If `kasan_mem_to_shadow()` gets called in an assembly file, it will use the default definition from the generic implementation. The addition of `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET` to the shifted address also warrants close scrutiny.  Because the offset is added to a `void*`, addition involves byte-level arithmetic. The C standard defines adding a large offset to a pointer that causes overflow as undefined behavior, and I checked to see that the `void*` arithmetic doesn't lead to undefined behavior.

So, the patch itself looks good in isolation. The logical shift implementation is elegant, and should maintain shadow memory consistency.     "thought": "**KASAN SW_TAGS and the Perilous Shift**

Okay, I'm diving into this KASAN code, specifically `kasan.h`.  I see the conditional logic for KASAN's shadow memory calculation.  If it's *not* generic KASAN, meaning we're dealing with Software Tags (SW_TAGS), an arithmetic shift is used. This seems... odd.  On ARM64, the arithmetic shift makes sense because of the tagged address structure where the top bits are all ones (canonical addresses). However, on x86, kernel addresses are sign-extended. Arithmetic shifts preserve this sign, which, in the context of tags, could lead to unexpected results, potentially with the tag bits.

I recall that x86 has a patch that explicitly forces a *logical* shift, as indicated in the static inline function `__kasan_mem_to_shadow`. Now, what happens if the input address `addr` *already* contains a tag? This is where things get tricky.  On x86, tags are 4 bits wide, occupying bits 57-60. These bits are part of the *canonical* address structure. It seems that the tag *overwrites* those canonical address bits.

The patch provides functions like `__tag_shifted`, which uses `GENMASK_ULL(60, 57)` to pinpoint the tag bits.  On x86, kernel pointers use bits 47..63, or 56..63 to encode the canonical information. The tag residing in bits 57..60 essentially makes a tagged pointer non-canonical!  The patch's comments mention it: the canonical address bits are being overwritten by the tag, the author mentions the canonical hooks being complex and that they are best avoided in the inline mode.

The patch's description also mentions using a *logical* shift, so here's what happens, in theory:  A tagged pointer `addr` has its tag in bits 57-60. Doing a *logical* right shift by 4 (as defined by `KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT`) moves those tag bits into bits 53-56.  Since the tag is *random* per allocation, the shadow memory address will vary randomly depending on the tag!

Let's do a concrete example to verify this assertion.  Assuming a valid kernel pointer `0xFFFF800000000000`, shifted right by 4 becomes `0x0FFF800000000000`, then add `KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET`, which is what generic KASAN uses.

Now, imagine we tag that same address with `0x5`.  The tag goes into bits 57-60.  `0x5` is `0101` in binary.  The tag sets bits 57-60.  The kernel address now has bits 57-60 as `0101`.  Then, when right-shifted by 4, the tag *shifts* into bits 53-56 of the shifted value! Okay, so I'm diving into this KASAN shadow address calculation. I see that the generic KASAN implementation uses `((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)`. But then I notice SW_TAGS KASAN is using `((long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)` instead. Hmm, why the difference? Why a *signed* right shift (`long`) instead of an *unsigned* one (`unsigned long`)? That feels wrong, but I'm sure there's a good reason.

Ah, it's because this is related to the arm64 architecture, and specifically SW_TAGS. The top byte is where the tag is stored. So `addr` might look something like `0xTXXXX...`. If we use an arithmetic right shift, it might be shifting in the sign bit, which would mess things up.

But wait, if we're using `(long)addr >> SHIFT` and the address *has* a tag, then what's even the *sign* bit? That's the real problem! On arm64, kernel addresses are typically `0xFFFF...` which means the top byte is `0xFF`. And the SW_TAGS scheme is putting the tag into that top byte, right?

Okay, okay… so if the tag is in the top byte, then bit 63 (the most significant bit) *could* be 0 or 1, depending on the tag's value! That is the catch.

Hold on. If bit 63 is 0, then an arithmetic shift will shift in *0s*! That's obviously going to produce an incorrect shadow address. That is bad.

Let me think… How is `addr` passed to `kasan_mem_to_shadow`? Is the tag cleared *before* it gets there? I need to check. Time to look at the callers of `kasan_mem_to_shadow` in `mm/kasan/`. Let's see what's really happening.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the KASAN SW_TAGS Patch: Arithmetic vs. Logical Shifts**

Okay, let's break this down. In `sw_tags.c`, `kasan_mem_to_shadow` is called with `untagged_addr`. I see the call chain. The initial thought is, why is the shift type even a consideration if it's operating on an untagged address? It doesn't seem to matter for the base functionality.

Ah, but the crux is understanding the implications on ARM64 versus x86, which is where the shift type difference becomes critical. On ARM64, kernel addresses use the top bit as a sign bit. An arithmetic right shift (`>>`) preserves this. A logical shift (`>>>`) clears it. Since ARM64 kernel addresses are of the format `0xFFFF...`, an arithmetic shift preserves those top bits, while a logical shift wouldn't. This seems to be the core reason why the original code seems fine on ARM64 as it preserves the bit.

The patch's explanation clarifies the X86 scenario: they *want* to use a logical shift for SW_TAGS on X86, because either the non-canonical hook becomes complex for X86 paging or inline mode becomes difficult. So, the patch provides an x86-specific implementation using a logical shift and the standard non-canonical shadow offset, adjusted for the increased 16-byte granularity. The x86 version looks like this:

```c
static inline void *__kasan_mem_to_shadow(const void *addr)
{
	return (void *)((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)
		+ KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
}
#define kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)	__kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)
```

Now, the question is, *does* `kasan_mem_to_shadow` ever get called with a *tagged* address on x86? This is important. In `sw_tags.c` it doesn't, but where else?

Looking at `mm/kasan/report_sw_tags.c` reveals this line at line 42: `while (p < end && tag == *(u8 *)kasan_mem_to_shadow(p))`.

Here, `p` *could* be tagged. Let's dig deeper into `mm/kasan/report_sw_tags.c` to see how that variable is being used.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Address Tagging in KASAN**

Okay, let's break this down. In `kasan_find_first_bad_addr`, that `kasan_reset_tag(addr)` call is a key point. It *untags* the address, meaning `p` is definitely untagged. Similarly, when we look at `kasan_get_alloc_size` and `kasan_metadata_fetch_row`, the `object` and `row` parameters, respectively, are typically passed from the cache, which means they're usually untagged as well. Hmm, in `kasan_print_tags`, that `addr` parameter... it *might* be tagged or untagged, right? But wait, let me remember - it's usually untagged. Let me check the call sites for `kasan_report` for confirmation.

The real question I need to answer is: **is there *any* scenario where `kasan_mem_to_shadow` actually receives a tagged address?** That's the crux of it. Because if it *does*, then the tag, which is positioned in those crucial bits (57-60 on x86, as defined by that `__tag_shifted` macro), would get shifted down by 4 bits. That means the tag's bits end up in the 53-56 position. The result? That *completely* messes with the shadow address calculation. We'd get the wrong shadow address. I need to dive into the call sites of `kasan_mem_to_shadow` and look for where that address might be tagged. Let's see...

    "thought": "**KASAN Tagging: A Deep Dive into Memory Shadowing**

Okay, so I'm looking into how KASAN handles memory tagging and shadowing, especially with the SW_TAGS mode. Let's start with `mm/kasan/shadow.c`. It's clear that `kasan_poison` and `kasan_unpoison` explicitly reset or remove the tag from the address, respectively. But, I need to understand if tagged addresses ever make their way into `kasan_mem_to_shadow`.

Aha! `mm/kasan/report.c` provides a key piece. It mentions that in SW_TAGS mode, the compiler *always* sets the kernel tag on the pointer *before* the shadow address is calculated. This is crucial! Specifically, it references `kasan_mem_to_shadow(tagged_null)` and `kasan_mem_to_shadow((void *)(~0ULL))`. I need to figure out how these tagged pointers are transformed to their corresponding shadow addresses.

`kasan_mem_to_shadow` uses a logical shift: `((unsigned long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)`. So what does this shift do to `~0ULL` which is `0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF`? Well, with a shift of 4 (if `KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT` is 4), it becomes `0x0FFFFFFFFFFFFFFF`. Also, wait, what is `tagged_null`? Let's check: it's `set_tag(NULL, 0)`.

Now, if we are on x86, which is what I'm interested in, SW_TAGS is in bits 57-60. The comment in `report.c` is important: the compiler sets the kernel tag before the mapping.

If the compiler does an arithmetic bit shift (the patch says this is an issue on x86), but the best working scheme uses a *logical* bit shift. This would mean that the compiler generates `(unsigned long)addr >> 4` or `KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT`.

So, if the original `addr` has a tag in bits 57-60, and we use a logical shift, that tag is shifted *into bits 53-56* of the shadow address. This is the critical insight: shadow addresses are now tag-dependent. Meaning that shadow addresses with different tags are *different*!

Let's read this again: `#define __tag_shifted(tag) FIELD_PREP(GENMASK_ULL(60, 57), tag)`.

On x86, kernel pointers have bits 47-63 set to 1 (in 4-level paging) or 56-63 set to 1 (in 5-level paging).

If `kasan_mem_to_shadow` uses the logical shift, with `KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT` as 4. A standard untagged kernel pointer, like `0xFFFF800000000000`, shifted right by 4, would give `0x0FFFF80000000000`.

If it *has* a tag, say `0x5`. In x86, the tag goes in bits 57-60.
Okay, so is `addr` really `0xFF5F800000000000`? No wait!
If bits 57-60 are the tag, let's see how `__tag_set` works.

Okay, so I'm diving into how KASAN's shadow memory addresses are computed, specifically with software tags. I was initially worried that different tags would lead to wildly different shadow addresses because the tag bits (bits 57-60, for example) are shifted down to the shadow address calculation, but it looks like I'm safe there.  The KASAN runtime, specifically the `kasan_mem_to_shadow` function, always operates on *untagged* addresses because it's meant to translate kernel pointers into their shadow memory locations, and the kernel guarantees the top byte, which is the kernel tag `0xFF`, is always set. When tag values other than `0xFF` are encountered, the code resets the tag to `0xFF` using `kasan_reset_tag` before calling the memory-to-shadow function. So, any pointer, regardless of any user-provided tag, ultimately gets the kernel tag before the shadow address is determined.

The core of the issue is in how the shift is implemented on x86, which is an important consideration because it impacts how the compiler instruments the code with inline checks for shadow memory.  I noticed the text mentioned that the patch fixes this by enforcing logical shift. The generic KASAN on x86 utilizes logical shift due to the non-canonical addresses for user and kernel space, ensuring a consistent shadow memory layout. If arithmetic shift were used, there would be sign extension issues. Because the kernel uses logical shift, the shadow offset calculations are based on logical shift, making the code consistent. The original thought was that `HWASAN` utilizes an arithmetic shift, and I had a bit of a panic.

Now, here's where it gets interesting: the compiler's behavior. The text indicates that the compiler, specifically LLVM's HWASAN pass, uses *logical* shift. If there was a mismatch in the shift type between the compiler and the kernel (e.g., compiler using logical shift, kernel using arithmetic shift), this would break the shadow address calculation.  I need to confirm if my current assumptions are true, because such a scenario would cause a significant bug by miscalculating the shadow addresses. 

The patch description also notes, "the inline mode would need a lot more adjustments".  What does that even mean?  When `CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS` is enabled with inline instrumentation, does the compiler insert checks using logical shift on x86? Is this what "the inline mode would need a lot more adjustments" refers to?

But, the code checks I examined use `LShr`, which is logical shift! Okay, the original text said that `KASAN_GENERIC` and `KASAN_SW_TAGS` shift differently.  Now, the main concern here is if HWASAN uses logical shift for *both* user and kernel addresses. In the kernel, the code falls into the `else` branch of `IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_KASAN_GENERIC)`, so it uses arithmetic shift in the memory-to-shadow mapping: `scaled = (void *)((long)addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT)`.

So it seems as though if the compiler uses `LShr` and the kernel uses arithmetic shift, this would be a real problem. So the concern is that there's a disconnect between the inline instrumentation generated by the compiler and the kernel runtime's shadow address calculations. Ultimately, I need to know why the kernel *chooses* arithmetic shift for tagged pointers, whereas the compiler uses logical shift. The kernel may choose arithmetic shift to handle kernel addresses. This could happen on arm64 and is not true for x86.

Author: Maciej Wieczor-Retman <maciej.wieczor-retman@intel.com>

x86/kasan: Use a logical bit shift for kasan_mem_to_shadow

This commit adds an arch-specific implementation of kasan_mem_to_shadow()
that uses a logical bit shift instead of an arithmetic one for tag-based
KASAN on x86. It also introduces tag manipulation macros for the architecture.

> diff --git a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
> index c868ae734f68..060ef997bfae 100644
> --- a/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
> +++ b/arch/x86/include/asm/kasan.h
> @@ -31,6 +31,21 @@
>  #include <linux/bits.h>
>  
>  #ifdef CONFIG_KASAN_SW_TAGS
[ ... ]
> +#define kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)	__kasan_mem_to_shadow(addr)
> +
> +#define __tag_shifted(tag)		FIELD_PREP(GENMASK_ULL(60, 57), tag)
> +#define __tag_reset(addr)		(sign_extend64((u64)(addr), 56))
> +#define __tag_get(addr)			((u8)FIELD_GET(GENMASK_ULL(60, 57), (u64)addr))

This isn't a bug, but does the addr parameter in __tag_get() need to be
enclosed in parentheses?

Without parentheses around addr, passing an expression like p + 1 expands
to (u64)p + 1. Due to C operator precedence, this casts p to a 64-bit integer
before adding 1, resulting in integer addition rather than pointer arithmetic.

It could also lead to compilation errors if the macro is used with
lower-precedence operators like the ternary operator. Wrapping it as
(u64)(addr) similar to __tag_reset() would prevent these macro expansion
issues.
